steps.title: Break down the claim
steps.content: The student's conclusion has two parts. First, the mere presence of anger may not hurt readers' perceptions of the review. Second, a high degree of anger does worsen those perceptions. Supporting data must therefore compare low anger and high anger with the no-anger control.

steps.title: Use the right measure
steps.content: The conclusion is about perceptions of the review, so the useful evidence is the helpfulness rating of the review, not participants' attitude toward the product. Low anger should look no worse than the control, while high anger should look worse than the control.

steps.title: Compare the choices
steps.content: Choice A compares review helpfulness with product ratings. Choice C adds product attitude, which is outside the conclusion. Choice D compares high anger only with low anger and does not use the no-anger baseline. The control-based pattern showing low anger slightly higher and high anger lower supports the student's conclusion. The answer is B.
